items.q: After one half-life, what happens to an individual radioactive nucleus?
items.correct: It has a 50 percent chance of having decayed — radioactive decay is random for any single nucleus.
items.misconception: Every nucleus is guaranteed to have half-decayed after one half-life.
items.random_wrong: The nucleus shrinks to half its original size after one half-life.
items.why: Students treat half-life as deterministic for individual atoms, when it is a statistical statement about large populations of nuclei.

items.q: Is radioactive decay periodic — does each nucleus decay at regular intervals?
items.correct: No — decay is a random process; half-life is a statistical property of a large population.
items.misconception: Yes — each nucleus 'ticks' at intervals of one half-life until it decays.
items.random_wrong: Yes — half-life is the time between each emission from the source.
items.why: Students treat half-life as a clock for individual atoms instead of a probability-based property of bulk samples.

items.q: Was the Big Bang an explosion of matter into pre-existing space?
items.correct: No — it was the expansion of space itself, beginning from a hot dense state; there was no 'outside' for matter to expand into.
items.misconception: Yes — it was a giant explosion at a point, and galaxies are still flying outwards from it.
items.random_wrong: Yes — it was a bomb-like event that destroyed an earlier universe.
items.why: Pop-science imagery of a fiery explosion encourages the wrong mental model; in fact every point in the universe was once 'the centre'.
The answer examiners want is: 4.5 x 10^5. “45 x 10^4” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's repeat measurements. “use better apparatus” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Ruler resolution 1 mm. % uncertainty in 20 mm reading?
items.why: The real figure is 2.5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
